calendar.matters:
Notice Of Hearing On Petition For Writ Of Mandate
Off Calendar Feb-17-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Marvin Lew (415-487-9100; marvin@daltonandlew.com) of Dalton & Lew LLP for Petitioner.
John McGlothlin (John.McGlothlin@doj.ca.gov), Deputy Attorney General for Respondent.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PETITIONER KEVIN CHEW's Petition For Writ Of Mandate:

Petitioner's petition for writ of administrative mandamus is denied.

The issues in the hearing before the administrative law judge ("ALJ") were whether (1) the officer had reasonable cause to believe the motorist drove under the influence of alcohol in violation of Vehicle Code section 23152 or 23153; (2) the motorist was placed under lawful arrest; (3) the motorist refused to submit to or failed to complete a chemical test after being requested by a peace officer; and (4) the motorist was admonished that his or her driving privilege would be suspended or revoked if he or she refused to submit to or failed to complete a chemical test. (Veh. Code, secs. 13353(d), 13557(a), (b)(l).)

Here, petitioner contests only the first element, i.e., whether Officer Smith had reasonable cause to pull petitioner over. Respondent DMV's findings bear a strong presumption of correctness and the burden rests upon petitioner to prove that the findings are contrary to the weight of the evidence. (See Fukuda v. City of Angels (1999) 20 Cal.4th 805, 817.)

A police officer may stop and detain for investigation a motorist on reasonable suspicion the driver has violated the law. (Ornelas v. U.S. (1996) 517 U.S. 690, 693.) This is a lesser standard than probable cause, as "an officer may stop and briefly detain a suspect for questioning for a limited investigation even if the circumstances fall short of probable cause to arrest." (Brierton v. Department of Motor Vehicles (2005) 130 Cal.App.4th 499, 509.) Officer Smith specifically articulated facts to support the detention.

Petitioner concedes that he crossed the white "fog line" once. The record further demonstrates-and petitioner does not contest-that he accelerated and travelled at a slow pace. (Petitioner's Ex. A [officer narrative]; Ex. D [Reporter's Transcript, pg. 5; Video]; People v. Russell (2000) 81 Cal.App.4th 96, 99 [slow speed supported detention]; Cornforth v. Department of Motor Vehicles (1970) 3 Cal.App.3d 550, 552 ["The slow speed was one factor which the officer reasonably could take into calculation."]; People v. Gibson (1963) 220 Cal.App.2d 15, 20 [driving slower than the speed limit is a factor that may justify an officer's investigation]).)

Petitioner relies on United States v. Gregory (10th Cir. 1996) 79 F.3d 973. The Court does not find that non-binding authority relating to a Utah statute compelling. The possibility that petitioner's driving was the result of an innocent explanation does not eclipse the specific and articulable facts that show reasonable cause for Officer Smith's stop. Driving over the fog line even once constitutes a violation of Vehicle Code section 21658(a) and Gregory did not involve slow driving. The slow driving and crossing the fog line were facts that supported the detention.

order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
